original_abstract: Abstract. The current understanding of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation within biomass burning (BB) plumes is limited by the incomplete identification and quantification of the non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) emitted from such fires. Gaseous organic compounds were collected on sorbent cartridges during laboratory burns as part of the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4) and analyzed by two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–ToFMS). The sensitivity and resolving power of GC × GC–ToFMS allowed the acquisition of the most extensive data set of BB NMOCs to date, with measurements for 708 positively or tentatively identified compounds. Estimated emission factors (EFs) are presented for these compounds for burns of six different vegetative fuels, including conifer branches, grasses, agricultural residue, and peat. The number of compounds meeting the peak selection criteria ranged from 129 to 474 among individual burns, and included extensive isomer groups. For example, 38 monoterpene isomers were observed in the emissions from coniferous fuels; the isomeric ratios were found to be consistent with those reported in relevant essential oils, suggesting that the composition of such oils may be very useful when predicting fuel-dependent terpene emissions. Further, 11 sesquiterpenes were detected and tentatively identified, providing the first reported speciation of sesquiterpenes in gas-phase BB emissions. The calculated EFs for all measured compounds are compared and discussed in the context of potential SOA formation.
humanized_abstract: Measurement of gas-phase organic compunds at FLAME-4 using GC×GC-ToFMS Abstract: There is currently limited understanding of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) processes occurring within biomass burning (BB) plumes largely due to a lack of knowledge regarding the primary non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) released by wildland fires. In this study, the gas-phase organic compounds collected on sorbent cartridges during laboratory burns were analyzed using two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–ToFMS). This GC × GC–ToFMS approach provided the most extensive data set of BB NMOCs to date with identifications of 708 positively or tentatively identified compounds using peak selection criteria. Emission factors (EFs) for these compounds are estimated and comparisons made among burns of six different vegetative fuels including: conifer branches, grasses, agricultural residue, peat and shrubs. The number of identified compounds per burn ranged from 129 to 474, with a large number of isomers identified in the emissions. For example, 38 different monoterpene isomers were identified in the coniferous fuel burns and these isomer ratios align with literature values of essential oils, suggesting that these compositions may be useful in modelling fuel-dependent terpenes emissions. Also, 11 sesquiterpenes were identified and characterized in the gas-phase BB emissions for the first time. The EFs for all identified compounds are provided and compared to explore the possible contributions of these compounds to SOA formation within BB plumes.

The current understanding of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation within biomass burning (BB) plumes is limited by the incomplete identification and quantification of the non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) emitted from such fires.
